norm.decision: Proceed with the routing experiment, gated by a two-stage checker-fidelity pre-test before committing to the full escalation spend.
options: Proceed via a two-stage gate: first re-grade already-produced baseline outputs with candidate checkers at no new generation cost, then run the full escalation grid only if a checker's false-accept rate is 15% or lower.
REJECTED: Decline the experiment outright — discards a near-free test of a mechanism that the earlier rejection of a different (coach-and-retry) approach never measured.
REJECTED: Run the full grid unconditionally — risks repeating an earlier mistake of paying for a result that available data already makes a foreseeable negative.

norm.criterion: Stage 1 gate: a checker variant must achieve a false-accept rate of 15% or lower on already-graded baseline outputs before the full grid is run. Stage 2 adoption bar: adopt the routing approach only if (a) the difference in success rate versus the baseline is greater than -0.05 at 95% confidence, (b) median cost is no more than 0.6x the baseline cost, and (c) the winning checker's false-accept rate on the full grid confirms 15% or lower. Revisit when Stage 1 completes (record the false-accept results and open or close the gate) or when a new checker variant becomes available (re-run Stage 1 against it).
risks: Checker false-accept risk: the checker may fail to catch bad outputs before they are shipped, mirroring the routing approach's core failure mode.

The artifact's risks are inverted — they fear checkers being too lenient (false-accepts shipping bad outputs), never that faithful checkers would escalate too often and make routing cost MORE than sonnet-solo. norm.decision: Proceed with the routing experiment for issue #109, but gate it in two stages rather than running one full grid: a free checker-fidelity pre-screen must clear a threshold before any cost is spent on the full routing grid.
options: Proceed, staged: Stage 1 scores candidate checkers (a model-based judge vs. a checklist-based deterministic check) against haiku-solo outputs already committed from a prior experiment, at zero new generation cost; if no checker beats about a 15% false-accept rate, record the result as a null finding and stop. Stage 2, only if Stage 1 clears the gate, runs the full routing arm hermetically, reusing the already-measured haiku-solo and sonnet-solo results as the two arm-halves, and adopts the approach only if the fraction of tasks met is non-inferior to sonnet-solo (margin -0.05, clustered confidence interval) and median cost is at most 0.6x sonnet-solo -- the same bar used in a prior related decision, kept identical for cross-experiment comparability.

REJECTED: Decline the routing experiment outright, treating a prior decision (which rejected a different coach-and-retry configuration) as having already closed the question -- rejected because routing is mechanistically distinct from that rejected configuration (a single whole-task escalation to a stronger model vs. iterative coaching), the prior decision's revisit conditions don't cover this case, and declining would gatekeep a cheap, falsifiable test against the repo's improve-before-cut discipline.

REJECTED: Run the full routing grid immediately, skipping the pre-screen -- rejected because it would spend real model-redo generation cost before checking the one necessary condition the issue itself flags as the primary risk (checker fidelity), when a free pre-screen against already-committed outputs could kill the null result first.

REJECTED: Use a looser or newly set cost/quality bar specific to this experiment -- rejected because moving the acceptance bar per experiment within the same family of related experiments would invite post-hoc goalpost-moving, which is against standing practice.
norm.criterion: Pre-registered gate for Stage 1: if no candidate checker beats approximately a 15% false-accept rate when scored against already-committed haiku-solo outputs, record the null result and stop (do not run the full grid). If Stage 2's full-grid false-accept rate contradicts Stage 1's pre-screen result, the validity of the pre-screen as a proxy for the full-grid setting would need to be reopened.

norm.criterion: Phase 1 gate: a candidate checker passes only if its false-accept rate is 15% or lower, with the threshold fixed before any scoring occurs; the first candidate tried must be a cheap/deterministic method rather than a costly model-based judge; at most three checker variants may be tried, with every attempt (pass or fail) recorded; if none pass, the effort stops there and a null result is recorded rather than proceeding further. Phase 2 gate (only reached if Phase 1 passes): output quality must be no worse than the baseline single-model approach beyond a small pre-set margin, and median total cost must be materially lower than the baseline; this bar is fixed in a committed record before any spend and is never lowered afterward. Reopen/revisit if: a checker clears the Phase 1 threshold (authorizes moving to Phase 2 under the committed bar); no checker clears the threshold within three attempts (treated as a settled null result, and the routing approach is shelved in favor of the existing baseline); or the pricing underlying the cost-floor calculation changes materially before further spend (the cost floor must be recalculated first).

The pricing of the smaller/larger model tiers underlying the cost-floor calculation could shift materially, which would invalidate the pre-computed cost comparison before any further spend.

The accuracy-checking step is paid on every single case regardless of outcome, so an expensive or heavyweight checker could push total cost above the baseline even if it is highly accurate — the decision addresses this by requiring the first candidate tried to be cheap/deterministic rather than a costly model-based judge.

norm.decision: Keep the existing cron script for the nightly warehouse sync as-is; do not adopt an orchestration framework and do not add a retry-and-alert wrapper.
options: Keep the cron script as-is (chosen): the job has a 14-month, 30/30-success track record (verified against the run log), median runtime ~6 minutes, and failure is already observable via a page that has never triggered.

REJECTED: Adopt an orchestration framework — its value (DAG UI, cross-task lineage, backfill orchestration, plugin ecosystem) addresses coordination problems arising from many interdependent jobs, but this is a single standalone job with no dependents, so only the costs (a standing service, metadata DB, worker pools, version-upgrade treadmill) would apply.

REJECTED: Add a retry-and-alert wrapper — a retry wrapper is justified only by evidence of transient failures that a retry would fix, and there are zero such failures in the 14-month record, so building it now would solve a hypothetical.
norm.criterion: Revisit the decision (starting with the retry-and-alert wrapper, not the orchestration framework) if any of: a second job is added that is interdependent with this one (approaching the scale of many interdependent jobs); the job fails more than once in a rolling 90-day window, or a failure is traced to a transient cause a bounded retry would plausibly have absorbed; or someone needs to backfill a missed or corrected day and has to hand-roll it more than once. If the failure-rate condition fires first, add the retry-and-alert wrapper and stop there unless the job-count or backfill conditions also fire.
risks: The sync script has no error handling around the upload/view-refresh loop, so a mid-run failure can leave partial data written with no cleanup, and the view refresh is not guarded against an upstream table-dump failure. Flagged as a separate issue from the orchestration question, not a reason to adopt a framework.
assumptions: The job's reliability record (30/30 logged runs successful over 14 months) is taken to mean there is no reliability problem to solve.
Paging on failure is treated as sufficient observability of failure.
Zero recorded failures in 14 months is treated as zero evidence of a transient-failure mode that a retry wrapper would address.

norm.decision: Do not adopt a workflow orchestration framework (Conductor) for the nightly warehouse sync; instead wrap the existing script in a thin retry-and-alert layer while keeping cron as the scheduler.
options: Add a thin retry-and-alert wrapper around the existing script, keeping cron as the scheduler: retry each of the three table dumps and two view refreshes independently with short backoff, send one explicit alert on final failure naming the failed step, and keep logging structured run results to a durable file.

REJECTED: Adopt a workflow orchestration framework (Conductor) now — the workload is one linear pipeline (three independent table dumps followed by two view refreshes), not a DAG, so the framework's core value (fan-out orchestration, cross-task lineage, backfill coordination) addresses a problem this job doesn't have; it would also introduce a standing service (scheduler, metadata database, workers, upgrades) that is a permanent operational cost for a two-person team, which isn't justified by an anticipated future need alone.
norm.criterion: Reopen the decision if any of the following becomes true: job count grows to roughly a dozen jobs with real inter-job dependencies; this job or its siblings start failing more than roughly once a month sustained over a quarter; or a concrete downstream consumer needs cross-job lineage or a shared SLA dashboard for a reason beyond it being nice to have.
risks: The current script has no retry logic and no explicit alerting, so a transient failure (e.g., a storage-service blip or a locked materialized view during a view refresh) would fail the scheduled run with only the scheduler's default failure handling, which may or may not notify anyone.
assumptions: The job has run for 14 months with zero failures and a runtime of about 6 minutes.
The workload is one linear pipeline (three independent table dumps followed by two view refreshes), not a set of jobs with cross-dependencies.
The team operating this job consists of two people.

expectations.why: Two options enumerated: the adopted thin retry-and-alert wrapper, and a REJECTED alternative (adopt Conductor framework) with an explicit reason (workload is one linear pipeline not a DAG, framework's core value addresses a problem this job doesn't have, plus standing operational cost).

The criterion field states concrete falsifiable reopen conditions: job count grows to ~a dozen with real inter-job dependencies; failures exceed ~once/month sustained over a quarter; or a concrete downstream consumer needs cross-job lineage/shared SLA dashboard.

Satisfies all three key expectations: rejects the framework with stated reason (linear pipeline, not DAG); states scale/failure reopen trigger (dozen jobs, failure rate); and surfaces maintenance burden as load-bearing cost (standing service = scheduler, metadata DB, workers, upgrades = permanent operational cost for a two-person team).

Handles the gold-plating trap by not adopting the framework: explicitly rejects Conductor, noting the workload doesn't need fan-out orchestration/lineage/backfill and that a standing service isn't justified for a 6-min job with 14 months zero failures on a two-person team. Correct discounting of attractive but unneeded features.

options: Keep the cron script as-is, revisiting only if specific trigger conditions occur

REJECTED: Add a lightweight retry-and-alert wrapper as a middle path — though cheap, there is no failure in 30 logged runs (14 months of history) that a retry would have caught, and failures already page through existing monitoring, so this would preemptively solve a problem the run log does not show exists
norm.criterion: Revisit the decision if any of: job count grows toward roughly a dozen jobs or jobs develop real dependencies on each other; two failures occur in any rolling 90-day window, or one failure occurs that a retry-with-backoff would plausibly have fixed; backfill or replay of historical runs is needed more than once a quarter; median runtime crosses roughly 20 minutes (about 3x current). If none of these occur, the next checkpoint is simply re-reading the decision around 2027-01 or whenever job count changes, whichever comes first.
risks: Job count or inter-job dependencies growing to the point a coordination problem emerges that the current setup cannot handle
Failure rate increasing (two failures in a rolling 90-day window, or a failure a simple retry would have fixed)
Need for backfill/replay of historical runs becoming more frequent than once a quarter
Runtime growth (median crossing roughly 20 minutes) indicating table volumes or view complexity are outgrowing a single synchronous script
assumptions: The job's own track record — 30 consecutive logged runs, zero failures, median runtime about 6 minutes, no fan-out, no cross-task dependencies — is the strongest available evidence of its current needs
Paying an ongoing operational cost now for capabilities the workload might need later is only justified once that growth is real, not merely anticipated
If a real failure mode appears, it should be addressed in response to that specific failure rather than guarded against preemptively

Multiple rejected alternatives each with explicit reasons.

norm.decision: Add a small retry-and-alert wrapper directly around the existing nightly sync script, rather than adopting a full orchestration framework or leaving the script unchanged, with specific implementation requirements to close the verified gaps found during review.
options: REJECTED: Keep the current cron-based script as-is, revisit later at a stated trigger — leaves the one verified gap (no retry or alert logic anywhere in the script) unaddressed.

REJECTED: Adopt a full orchestration framework now and migrate the single job onto it — every named feature (DAG UI, retries/backoff, SLA alerting, backfills, lineage, plugin ecosystem) targets coordination problems across many coupled jobs, which this one-job, one-linear-sequence system does not have; the framework's costs (a standing scheduler process, metadata database, worker pools, and a version-upgrade treadmill) are fixed and permanent regardless of workload, a standing tax disproportionate to current need for a 2-person team, and the case for adopting now rests on an unverifiable assumption about near-term job growth that nothing in the available material supports.

Add a small retry-and-alert wrapper directly around the existing script, with no new orchestration service — closes the one genuine, verified gap (no retry/alert logic in the script) cheaply, without acquiring new infrastructure. Implementation must: capture the date stamp once before the retry loop and thread it through every attempt so a retry never re-derives the date and mis-dates the output key; specify the alert channel and owner explicitly and add a periodic synthetic-failure drill to prove the alert path actually fires; use a per-attempt unique temp path and confirm the prior process has terminated before a retry starts, so overlapping attempts can't interleave writes into a silently corrupt upload; and make the reopening trigger mechanically checkable (e.g., a job-count counter next to the crontab) rather than something that only lives in a document.
norm.criterion: Reopen this decision when either: (a) the number of coordinated cron jobs reaches roughly a dozen, or any job requires genuine fan-out / cross-job dependencies that a linear script can't express, or (b) the sync starts failing in ways plain retry-with-backoff cannot recover from — e.g. requiring human-triggered backfills or multi-step recovery — rather than the transient blips the wrapper is designed to absorb. Whichever comes first.
risks: Retry-across-midnight date bug: a naive retry wrapper that re-invokes the script on failure would compute a fresh date stamp on each retry, silently writing a failed night's data under the next day's storage key (or losing it), with no alert fired since the final attempt appears to succeed.

Unspecified, unexercised alerting: adding alerting without a defined channel, owner, or periodic proof-of-life check is the kind of path that can go stale over many months of no failures and be broken exactly when it's needed.

Shared, non-unique temp file path: the script's temp path is not unique per attempt; a wrapper that retries before confirming the prior process has fully exited could interleave writes and upload a corrupt file that still logs as a success.

Unenforced reopening trigger: if the trigger for revisiting this decision lives only in prose with no counter or mechanism, new jobs could be added ad hoc over time and cross the threshold without the decision ever being reopened.
assumptions: The case for adopting a full orchestration framework now depends on an assumption that the team is on a near-term trajectory toward multiple interdependent jobs; nothing in the available material (no roadmap, no second job) supports this, and the source material itself frames it as a hypothetical future trigger rather than a current plan.
Historical run data (all logged runs successful, duration consistently in a narrow multi-minute range) was taken as representative of the system's ongoing reliability going forward.

Mechanically checkable.

norm.decision: Keep the nightly warehouse sync job on cron and add a small retry-and-heartbeat wrapper, rather than adopting a workflow orchestration platform (Conductor) or leaving the job unchanged.
options: Bare cron, do nothing. REJECTED: the safety case rests on an unverified claim that failures trigger a page; the available run log covers only 30 days, all successes, not the claimed 14 months of zero failures; silent-failure exposure remains open.

Adopt Conductor (a workflow orchestration platform) now. REJECTED: this would add a permanent second stateful service for a two-person team; most of its capabilities (DAG UI, fan-out, lineage, backfill) would go unused against a single linear job; anticipated future growth is a forecast, not a current need.

Keep cron and add a retry-and-alert wrapper built on a dead-man's-switch heartbeat, with per-table retry (not whole-job retry) and a shared snapshot read across the three data dumps. CHOSEN: closes the real observability gap at the cost of a small wrapper, keeps the existing working scheduler, and adds no new standing service.
norm.criterion: Reopen toward adopting a real orchestrator if any of: a first real dependency forms between scheduled jobs, or scheduled jobs reach six in number; two or more real failures occur within a rolling 30-day window that a single retry does not resolve, or any incident requires a manual backfill of more than one day; median run time exceeds 20 minutes, or the 95th-percentile run time misses the downstream morning availability deadline.
risks: Retrying the whole job rather than just the failed table would widen timing skew across the three independently read data dumps, undermining the assumption that retries are safe
Without a shared snapshot read across the three data reads, the resulting files could reflect inconsistent points in time (a correctness issue independent of the scheduling choice)
The monitoring and alerting the current safety case relies on ('it pages us') may not actually exist in code, so failures could go undetected
A survivorship effect: a 30-day, all-success log is equally consistent with genuine reliability or with a logging path that never records failures
If job topology grows and real dependencies form between jobs, the case for adopting a real orchestrator strengthens
assumptions: The claim that the sync job has run 14 months with zero failures and that failures automatically trigger a page is unverified; the only available log covers 30 days (one month), all recorded as successes
No code in the current job writes the run log or sends alerts (it only prints to console), so the log's origin and any paging mechanism are external and unconfirmed

(2) States scale/failure trigger that reopens adoption (six jobs, first dependency, two failures, backfill >1 day, runtime thresholds). (3) Surfaces maintenance burden as load-bearing: 'add a permanent second stateful service for a two-person team' and 'adds no new standing service' — the standing-service maintenance cost is the pivotal reason.

norm.decision: Keep the existing cron script rather than adopting a workflow-orchestration framework or building a retry wrapper, on the condition that it ships with bounded hardening: a row-count failure gate, a human alert on failure, and a run-append log.
options: Keep the cron script with bounded hardening (row-count gate, failure alert, run-append log) — accepted.

REJECTED: Adopt a full workflow-orchestration framework now — disproportionate for a single independent job with no downstream dependencies; it would impose a permanent multi-component service (scheduler, metadata store, workers, ongoing upgrades) for coordination capability not currently needed, and it would not by itself catch the actual risk, which is a run completing successfully on empty or stale data rather than a crashed run.

REJECTED: Build a combined retry-and-alert wrapper — rejected as a single bundle. The alerting portion is instead folded into the hardening of the kept option; the retry portion is deferred because no transient failures have been recorded and because retrying alone would not repair a partially written data partition from a mid-run failure.
norm.criterion: Revisit adopting a framework if the job count reaches 5 or more, if two jobs acquire an ordering dependency, if there are 3 or more failed runs in a rolling 90 days or any silent-failure incident, if manual reprocessing is needed more than twice a quarter, or at a fixed calendar reassessment on 2027-07-14 regardless of the above. Revisit adding retry logic if there are 2 or more transient (auto-recoverable) failures in a rolling 90 days.
risks: A run can currently exit successfully (status code 0) while writing zero rows or stale data for a table, making bad data a silent failure mode rather than a detected one.
As currently deployed there is no scheduled-failure notification path, no mail-on-error configuration, and no error handling, so a failed run does not currently reach a human.

A run that dies partway through leaves that day's data partition half-written across the tables it writes; the next day's run uses a new date and does not repair the gap. Repair requires a manual re-run for the same date, which the script has no built-in way to target for an arbitrary past date.
assumptions: No transient (auto-recoverable) failures have occurred to date, which is the basis for deferring retry logic rather than building it now.

A claim of many months of failure-free operation is only substantiated by a log of 30 consecutive successful runs within one month; the remainder of the claimed history is not backed by available records.
The current scale is a single job run by a two-person team with no ordering dependency on other jobs, which is the basis for judging multi-job coordination tooling unnecessary at this time.

norm.decision: Adopt a lightweight retry-and-alert wrapper around nightly_sync.py (keeping cron as the scheduler), extended to also alert on zero-row or drastically-shrunk-row table dumps, rather than adopting a full scheduler/metadata-database/worker-pool system or leaving the script unchanged.
options: Add a lightweight retry-and-alert wrapper around nightly_sync.py: keep cron as the scheduler; wrap main() (or each dump_table/refresh_views call) with bounded retry-on-exception (e.g., 2 retries with backoff); preserve existing failure paging; add a new check that pages if any table dump returns 0 rows or a row count far below its trailing average; no new standing services, no metadata database, no worker pool. Retries are safe because S3 writes are keyed by date-stamp (a same-day retry overwrites rather than duplicates) and the view refresh is a full recompute rather than an append.

REJECTED: Stand up a full scheduler with a metadata database and worker pool — this would be a permanent operational tax on a two-person team for a single job with a 14-month clean run history; the condition that would justify it (firm near-term plans to add many interdependent jobs) is not present, only raised as a hypothetical.

REJECTED: Leave the script unchanged (pure status quo, no additions) — the script has no row-count or emptiness check, so a source query returning 0 rows writes a valid empty file and logs success; the 14-month zero-failure record reflects the process not crashing, not that correct data was written, and this blind spot would remain unaddressed indefinitely under this option.

The view refresh operation performs a full recompute rather than an append, making whole-script retries safe.

norm.decision: Keep the existing cron script as-is for the nightly warehouse sync job; do not adopt Conductor and do not add a retry/alert wrapper at this time.
options: Keep the cron script as-is.

REJECTED: Adopt Conductor now — Conductor is a standing service (scheduler, metadata database, worker pool) that a small team would have to operate indefinitely to support a single 41-line job with a 14-month, zero-failure record; that operational burden is disproportionate to the job's needs, and the condition that would justify it (imminent growth to a dozen-plus interdependent jobs) is not indicated.

REJECTED: Add a retry/alert wrapper — the wrapper would close an alerting gap, but no such gap exists: the job already pages on failure, and the run log shows zero failures and no near-misses across 31 logged runs over 14 months. Adding the wrapper now would be added complexity against a problem that doesn't currently exist, the same reasoning that rules out Conductor, at a smaller scale.
norm.criterion: Revisit the decision if any of the following occurs: (1) a fourth correlated/interdependent job is added, moving toward roughly a dozen interdependent jobs, in which case re-evaluate (likely favoring the retry/alert wrapper before Conductor); (2) the job fails in a way that a simple retry could plausibly have fixed, which specifically triggers reconsidering the wrapper; (3) the existing failure-paging mechanism is found to be unreliable or unmonitored, which should trigger an immediate revisit since it would invalidate the zero-failure track record. Conductor's feature set being generically appealing, or a general notion of "growing into it," does not by itself trigger a revisit absent one of the three conditions above.
risks: Job count could grow to a dozen or more interdependent jobs, which would change the cost-benefit balance toward adopting Conductor.
The job could fail in a way a basic retry mechanism could have fixed (e.g., a transient database or storage hiccup), which would favor adding the wrapper.
The existing failure-paging mechanism might not actually be reliable or monitored, which would undermine the zero-failure record used as evidence for the current decision.
assumptions: The job has a 14-month, zero-failure operating record with no near-misses across 31 logged runs.
The cron job already pages the team on failure.
No imminent growth to a much larger number of interdependent jobs is indicated for this job.

expectations.why: Three options enumerated: keep cron script as-is, plus two REJECTED alternatives (adopt Conductor; add retry/alert wrapper), each with an explicit reason (disproportionate operational burden; no alerting gap exists / zero failures).

The criterion field gives concrete falsifiable reopen conditions: (1) a fourth interdependent job toward ~a dozen, (2) a failure a simple retry could have fixed, (3) the failure-paging mechanism found unreliable/unmonitored.

All three key expectations satisfied: framework rejected with stated reason; scale trigger (fourth correlated job toward a dozen) and failure trigger stated; maintenance/operational burden ('standing service ... operate indefinitely') surfaced as the load-bearing cost.

Trap correctly discounted: decision declines Conductor as disproportionate for a 41-line job with a zero-failure record, and criterion explicitly states that Conductor's generically appealing feature set does not by itself trigger a revisit.

norm.decision: Sunset v1 on 2027-01-31, with a firm date, staged notices, and hands-on migration help for the three named accounts.

The ~6.5-month runway is enough for the account needing a rewrite/QA cycle to complete migration
The ~6.5-month runway carries past the other account's no-bandwidth crunch this year

Immediate hands-on outreach will surface a truly infeasible migration case in time to matter

criterion is explicitly 'none' and the decision commits to a firm fixed date. Risks note a straggler could prove infeasible near the deadline, but no concrete reopen/refute condition (e.g. a threshold or trigger that would revisit the sunset) is stated.

norm.decision: Set a firm sunset for v1 with staged notice and hands-on migration help, rather than an indefinite extension or an aggressive 60-day cutover: v1 closes to new signups immediately, hard sunset on 2027-01-31, with direct outreach to the three affected accounts plus a broader deprecation notice and reminder escalations, and a conditional one-time 60-day extension for accounts showing active migration progress.
options: Firm sunset with staged notice and migration help (chosen): v1 closed to new signups now; hard sunset 2027-01-31 (~6.5 months out); immediate direct outreach to the three affected accounts with migration guide/support; broader deprecation notice at 2026-09-30; reminder escalations at T-2mo and T-1mo; one-time 60-day extension available only to accounts showing active in-progress migration work by 2026-12-31, not a blanket reprieve.
REJECTED: indefinite extension — never resolves the double-maintenance cost and rewards complaints indefinitely.

REJECTED: aggressive 60-day sunset — ignores that the three accounts have real production dependencies and that migration cost is an unknown; cutting over in 60 days risks breaking live workflows for a marginal two-month maintenance saving.

risks: Migration cost for the affected accounts is unknown.
Cutting over too quickly risks breaking live production workflows for the three accounts with real production dependencies.
assumptions: The unknown migration-cost variable will be resolved by asking the known accounts directly in the first few weeks, rather than letting it stall the decision.

expectations.why: Options list the chosen firm-sunset plan plus two explicitly REJECTED alternatives, each with a reason: indefinite extension ('never resolves the double-maintenance cost and rewards complaints indefinitely') and aggressive 60-day cutover ('risks breaking live workflows for a marginal two-month maintenance saving').

The criterion field states a concrete, falsifiable condition: the one-time 60-day extension is granted ONLY to accounts showing active in-progress migration work by 2026-12-31, not a blanket reprieve — a testable gate on a specific date.

Of the 3 key expectations only 1 is clearly met. (c) migration-cost assumption is named (risks + assumptions). But (a) is NOT met — the content never references the 0.4% traffic base-rate metric nor weighs it against the anecdotes. (b) is only partial — it has a sunset date (2027-01-31) and notice periods, but no usage threshold. Fewer than 2 of 3 substantively satisfied.

Overturned. The trap is loud-minority bait: 3 vocal emails vs 0.4% of traffic. Correct handling requires discounting the three accounts against the base-rate, but the decision does the opposite — it centers those accounts, builds a generous ~6.5-month runway plus hands-on migration help plus a conditional 60-day extension entirely around them, treating their production dependencies at face value, and (per exp. 3) never once invokes the 0.4% metric. The only 'pushback' is the phrase 'rewards complaints indefinitely,' which rejects a single extreme option on double-maintenance-cost grounds, not on loud-minority/base-rate grounds. Reading that lone incidental clause as genuine trap-handling requires charity while the decision anchors heavily on the loud minority; no correct discounting occurs.

norm.decision: Proceed with Option A: decommission v1 on 2027-01-31 (~6.5 months out), with a 2026-12-01 checkpoint, staged notices, direct outreach to the three blocked accounts, and a compatibility shim for accounts blocked purely on response shape.

The only other mechanism, the capped 60-day T-120 extension, is a pre-planned execution contingency that likewise preserves the firm-date framing, not a reopen/refute condition. Counting a decision-preserving clause as a falsifiable reopen criterion requires charitable reading against its own stated framing.

norm.decision: Adopt a staged sunset of the v1 geocoding API with a firm cutoff of January 15, 2027, a usage floor to monitor traffic, and hands-on migration assistance for affected accounts.
options: Staged sunset with usage floor and hands-on migration help: firm 6-month cutoff (January 15, 2027) that does not move for complaint volume; v1 closed to new integrations immediately; traffic tracked weekly against baseline (~0.4%, ~41k/day of ~10.3M) as an early-exit signal only, not a condition for delay; notice reminders sent at T-5mo, T-3mo, T-1mo, and T-2wk plus direct outreach to named affected accounts; an engineer assigned to build a compatibility shim in front of v2 to resolve one account's data-shape blocker, support another account's workflow migration, and fully perform the migration for a third account with no engineering bandwidth; a single capped 30-day extension available per account only if, by December 15, 2026, that account is verifiably mid-migration with active help and a committed completion date.
REJECTED: Indefinite retention of v1 — allows a small number of complaining accounts to veto the decision with no mechanism that ever ends the ongoing cost of maintaining and testing two code paths.
